summary:
The Swiss Confederation (founded by the Federal Charter of 1291) was a defensive alliance of three forest cantons — Uri, Schwyz (which gave Switzerland its name), and Unterwalden — around the shores of Lake Lucerne that grew through the 14th and 15th centuries into a confederation of eight cantons and eventually the modern Swiss state. The original alliance was formed to resist the expanding power of the Habsburg dynasty, and the Swiss repeatedly demonstrated that their pike-armed infantry could defeat the supposedly invincible armored cavalry of the Austrian and Burgundian nobility.

The Swiss military system was revolutionary in late medieval Europe. Swiss soldiers fighting in dense pike squares — rectangular formations of men armed with 5-6 meter pikes and halberds, trained to maneuver aggressively — proved capable of defeating armored cavalry charges and became the most sought-after mercenaries in Europe. The Battle of Morgarten (1315), where Swiss troops ambushed and slaughtered an Austrian cavalry force in a mountain pass; Laupen (1339); and Sempach (1386) established Swiss military reputation. The victories over Charles the Bold of Burgundy at Grandson, Murten, and Nancy (1476–1477) — some of the most decisive battles of the 15th century — made Swiss pikemen the dominant military force in Europe and established Switzerland as effectively independent of any external power. Swiss mercenaries (Reisläufer) became indispensable to every major European power; the tradition survives in the Papal Swiss Guard, founded in 1506. The Swiss Confederation's success in maintaining independence and developing proto-republican cantonal governance made it an inspiration for later republican political thinking.
